retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Some RNAs also function as catalytic RNA to drive biochemical reactions; hence they are termed ribozymes. The ribozymes also sometimes pair with auxiliary proteins to carry out their catalytic functions. The biochemical reactions catalyzed by ribozymes include protein synthesis, RNA splicing, and RNA cleavage. Ribozymes fold into a complex tertiary structure to form an active site that allows nucleophilic substitution reactions for phosphoryl transfer. These reactions are facilitated through acid-base catalysis with the involvement of ribonucleosides and metal ions. [8] Ribozymes classify into two types: small ribozymes (eg- hairpin, hammerhead, and Hepatitis delta virus) and large ribozymes (group I and II introns, RNase P, spliceosome, and the ribosomes).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: RNA viruses can survive and disrupt physiological processes by replicating their genome inside a host cell. There are different kinds of RNA viruses, and each has a unique mechanism of replication. Double-stranded RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, fuse with host cell membranes and inject their viral contents inside to replicate their genome via reverse transcription. Reverse transcription requires a ribonuclease H and a DNA polymerase with the ability to copy an RNA or a DNA template to synthesize a linear double-stranded DNA from an RNA. [19] Positive-stranded RNA viruses have a positive virion RNA that already acts as an mRNA and, therefore, can be translated immediately by RNA polymerase. Negative-stranded RNA viruses have a negative virion RNA that is complementary to mRNA and must be copied into a positive-sense mRNA before translation. Viral proteins are then released from the host cell to infect other cells, consequently interfering with normal biological processes.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Three main types of RNA are involved in protein synthesis. They are messenger RNA (mRNA), transfer RNA (tRNA), and ribosomal RNA (rRNA).

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Structure -- Cargo
retrieved_docs.content: EVs may contain any content found in their origin cell’s cytoplasm; this includes proteins, mRNA, miRNA, long noncoding RNAs (lncRNA), circular RNAs (circRNA), metabolites, and lipids. [12] [13] . Some research showed an association between DNA and EVs. [14] [15] Furthermore, some APBs contain intact cell organelles like mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and ribosomes. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- MicroRNA
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNA) are non-coding RNAs mainly involved in gene regulation. They are mostly processed from introns and are transcribed into primary miRNA from the host gene by RNA polymerase II. They are then modified by endonucleases, such as Drosha and Dicer, into mature miRNA. Studies have shown that miRNAs that bind to an untranslated region (3’UTR) on mRNAs suppress translation, while miRNA binding to promoter regions can upregulate transcription. [13] miRNAs can also function similarly to hormones. They are released into the extracellular fluid and taken up by target cells for regulation of cellular activity. Additionally, researchers are studying these extracellular miRNAs as ideal biomarkers for various diseases. Research has already shown circulating miRNAs to be involved in cancer through their role in controlling oncogenes and tumor suppressors. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of RNA is to create proteins via translation. RNA carries genetic information that is translated by ribosomes into various proteins necessary for cellular processes. mRNA, rRNA, and tRNA are the three main types of RNA involved in protein synthesis. RNA also serves as the primary genetic material for viruses. Other functions include RNA editing, gene regulation, and RNA interference. These processes are carried out by a group of small regulatory RNAs, which include small nuclear RNA, microRNA, and small interfering RNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Besides the most important function of RNA to make proteins, other critical cellular functions include modifying and restructuring other RNAs and regulating gene expressions during growth and development, and changing cellular environments.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: RNA sequencing has the potential to be an efficient clinical diagnostic tool because of its ability to measure gene expression outside the physiological range, allele-specific expression, and defects in alternative splicing. [18] Sequencing RNA involves isolating the RNA, preparation of an RNA library, and utilization of next-generation sequencing technology. The transcriptome can then undergo analysis for any aberrant genes.

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen is a 20-base pair, 2’-O-methoxyethyl modified antisense oligonucleotide complementary to human apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA. [3] Mipomersen aims to identify and hybridize with the human apolipoprotein B-100 mRNA via the traditional deoxyribonucleotide or ribonucleotide base pairing mechanism. The interface between the complementary mipomersen therapeutic oligonucleotide with the newly synthesized human apolipoprotein B-100 leads to the degradation of the messenger RNA. Most frequently, the hybridization of mipomersen to its target mRNA leads to the recruitment of RNase H1, a catalytic enzyme that degrades ribonucleic acid in the cellular cytoplasm. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: siRNAs additionally regulate gene expression. In one study, pseudogene transcripts were observed to form hairpin structures through folding and become functional siRNAs that repressed gene expression. The study additionally removed Dicer, a protein responsible for producing siRNAs, and observed a decreased concentration of pseudogene-derived siRNAs with subsequent increased expression of the coding gene’s mRNA products. The study further supports the idea of pseudogene derived siRNA regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Novel Therapies [4] [24] [31] [32] Antisense Oligonucleotides (AONs): work by degrading the CUG expansion, or by binding to CUG expansion to inhibit RNA sequestration and sites for abnormal MBNL binding. Recombinant Adeno-associated viral (rAAV): stimulates overexpression of MBNL1, to prevent sequestration. Inhibition of CUG-BP1 activity via small molecules (pentamidine) or by inhibiting protein kinase C (involved in activating CUG-BP1) can also prevent sequestration. Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR/Cas): cleave and degrade CUG mRNA expansion. Others: agents to increase muscle anabolism, such as testosterone, creatine, dehydroepiandrosterone, and recombinant insulin-like growth factor (IGF-1), and myostatin inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Molecular Level -- What is the composition of RNA?
retrieved_docs.content: The primary structure of RNA is composed of nucleotides attached by 5’-3’ phosphodiester bonds between ribose sugars. Ribose has the molecular formula C5H10O5 and has a naturally occurring D-ribose form and a less common L-ribose. The D and L designations refer to the hydroxyl group positions. The nucleotide bases consist of adenine, guanine, cytosine, and uracil. Two hydrogen bonds form between adenine and uracil, while three bonds form between cytosine and guanine. The base pairing via hydrogen bonds is the basis of RNA's secondary structure. The RNA tertiary structure is the result of RNA folding, which creates a three-dimensional shape consisting of helices and grooves. RNA differs from DNA in that it contains a uracil nucleotide instead of thymine and carries a 2’ hydroxyl group rather than a 2’ hydrogen. Due to its interaction with the solvent environment, the 2’ hydroxyl group contributes to RNA conformation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A major concern is RNA mutations, which can disrupt the normal functioning of RNA and cause potentially life-threatening diseases. RNA errors can be the result of defects in the ribonucleoprotein complex, RNA itself, RNA binding proteins, or any RNA assembly factors. Myotonic dystrophy is a neuromuscular disease that is caused by a CTG nucleotide repeat on the DMPK gene resulting in a pathogenic RNA gain-of-function. [3] A mutation in splicing can result in a mutated SMN2 gene and lead to spinal muscular atrophy. [4] Other concerning illnesses caused by RNA mutations include Prader-Willi syndrome, prostate cancer, fragile X syndrome, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, RNA viruses can also recombine and reassort with DNA and RNA from the host or other viral strains, potentially generating a newer strain. Influenza viruses have a very high ability to reassort; for example, the H1N1 influenza strain recombined with the RNA segments from birds, humans, and pig viruses to generate the H1N1 strain that caused a pandemic in 2009. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonukleasy, an enzyme that catalyzes the cleavage of DNA strands, encompasses within its broader class the more specific enzyme streptodornase; streptodornase represents a narrower subclass of deoxyribonukleasy with distinct functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 397
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is chemically related to depsipeptidy, indicating that it belongs to or is associated with the broader class of depsipeptide compounds. In addition, Actinomycin functions as a DNA minor groove binding agent, placing it within the category of agents that specifically bind to the minor groove of DNA to interfere with transcription and replication processes. This dual characterization highlights both its structural classification as a depsipeptide‑related molecule and its pharmacological role in targeting DNA architecture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin, a therapeutic agent that promotes fibrinolysis by breaking down fibrin clots. By incorporating the enzymatic activities of both deoxyribonucleases and plasmin, this formulation leverages plasmin’s natural ability to cleave fibrin and deoxyribonucleases’ capacity to degrade nucleic acids, enhancing the overall efficacy of fibrinolysin in clinical settings where clot dissolution is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: oligonucleotide therapy to its broader preparation group. Understanding these classifications, mechanisms (direct versus peripheral action), chemical families, formulation details, and safety relationships is essential for clinicians to select appropriate agents, anticipate therapeutic effects, and manage potential side effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 830
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are classified as direct acting antivirals, indicating that this group of cyclic amine compounds belongs to the broader class of antiviral agents that directly inhibit viral replication by targeting specific viral enzymes or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes, hematological are identified as members of both the deoxyribonuclease group and the trypsin group, indicating that these blood‑related enzymes are classified within two distinct enzyme families. As members of the deoxyribonuclease family, they possess nuclease activity capable of cleaving DNA, while their classification as members of the trypsin family reflects proteolytic functions typical of serine proteases. This dual categorization highlights the multifunctional nature of hematological enzymes, which can participate in both nucleic acid metabolism and protein digestion processes within the circulatory system, underscoring the importance of recognizing overlapping enzymatic roles in patient care and biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1706
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes, hematological is a classification that includes both chymotrypsin and deoxyribonukleasy, indicating that each of these proteins is a member of the hematological enzyme group. As members of this category, chymotrypsin functions as a proteolytic enzyme that cleaves peptide bonds, while deoxyribonukleasy participates in the degradation of nucleic acids. Recognizing chymotrypsin and deoxyribonukleasy as components of the Enzymes, hematological class helps clarify their biochemical roles in blood‑related processes and informs clinical considerations of disorders involving proteolysis or DNA breakdown.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1321
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is classified as a depsipeptide, indicating that it belongs to a broader chemical family of compounds that contain both peptide and ester linkages. This broader relationship situates actinomycin within a group of biologically active molecules whose structural features influence their pharmacological properties and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals and neuraminidase inhibitors, direct acting antivirals are both subclasses of direct acting antivirals. Rimantadine is a member of the cyclic amine subclass and produces the physiologic effect known as Cellular Synthetic Activity Alteration, indicating that it alters cellular synthetic activity. Zanamivir is a member of the neuraminidase inhibitor subclass, placing it within the broader category of direct acting antivirals. These connections show how individual antiviral agents such as rimantadine and zanamivir are organized within specific antiviral classes and highlight rimantadine’s mechanism of altering cellular synthetic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1705
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serve as the parent class for the therapeutic agent nusinersen, indicating that nusinersen is a specific preparation within this broader category. Nusinersen, in turn, produces increased protein synthesis as a physiologic effect, reflecting its mechanism of action. Understanding that nusinersen belongs to the N [Preparations] family and that it induces increased protein synthesis helps clarify its role in clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 616
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is classified as a hematological enzyme, belonging to the broader enzyme group Enzymes, hematological, which in turn is a subset of the larger family deoxyribonukleasy. Within deoxyribonukleasy, more specific members such as streptodornase are recognized, indicating that streptodornase represents a narrower subclass of these nucleic‑acid‑degrading enzymes. Clinically, Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of the human fibrinolysin preparation, highlighting its therapeutic role in promoting fibrinolysis. This hierarchical and functional information links the enzyme’s biochemical classification to its practical application in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.
